content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assuance · · Administration · · Executive/Legal · · Enforcement
(916) 322-5662          322-5660             322-5901          322-6441


July 27, 1984


Malcolm B. Hunter
City Attorney
City of Richmond
330 City Hall
Richmond, CA 94804

Re: Our Telephone Conversation
    File No. A-84-193

Dear Mr. Hunter:

    This letter is sent to confirm our telephone conversation of
July 23, 1984, in which you requested advice and information
concerning the conflict of interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act. You stated that there was a decision pending before
the Richmond City Council concerning the utility user tax and,
in particular, its application to a very large corporation. The
corporation would be subject to a different tax rate than others
because of a "cap" provision which would affect only the
corporation. One of the councilmembers has an investment of
more than $1,000 in this corporation. The possible financial
effect of the Council decision on the corporation was $1 million
to $2 million. I advised you that, based on these facts, the
councilmember had a conflict of interest within the meaning of
Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103 and Commission
regulations, 2 Cal. Adm. Code sections 18700 and 18702. He
should abstain from all participation in this matter.

    We also generally discussed the potential for conflicts of
interest in city taxing decisions. I explained to you that the
unique circumstances of each situation must be analyzed. In
some cases, the "public generally" exception contained in
Government Code Section 87103 may apply,1/ but, in most taxing

1/ Section 87103 provides that where the foreseeable,
material financial effect of a governmental decision on an
official's financial interest is indistinguishable from the
effect of the decision on the public generally, the official is
not prohibited from participating in the decision. We often
call this the public generally exception to disqualification.

Malcolm B. Hunter
July 27, 1984
Page 2


decisions, there are questions of rate structure where the
public generally exception clearly would not apply.

     For your further information, I have enclosed a copy of a
staff advice letter and a copy of the stipulation in the recent
Commission proceeding against Mayor Thomas J. Corcoran which
discusses the utility user tax imposed last fall (see
specifically pp. 16-18). If I can be of further assistance,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Diane Maura Fishburn
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

DMF:plh
Enclosures

sections.question_synthetic: Does a city councilmember with an investment in a corporation subject to a utility user tax decision have a conflict of interest that requires abstention from voting?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Yes, the councilmember has a conflict of interest under Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103 because they have a financial interest exceeding $1,000 in the corporation and the decision would have a material financial effect ($1-2 million). The councilmember must abstain from all participation. The 'public generally' exception typically does not apply to rate structure questions in taxing decisions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No formal QUESTION and CONCLUSION sections present; advice provided via telephone conversation and confirmed in letter format. Synthetic question and conclusion derived from body text. Includes footnote explanation of 'public generally' exception under Government Code Section 87103.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does a city councilmember with an investment in a corporation subject to a utility user tax decision have a conflict of interest that requires abstention from voting?

CONCLUSION: Yes, the councilmember has a conflict of interest under Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103 because they have a financial interest exceeding $1,000 in the corporation and the decision would have a material financial effect ($1-2 million). The councilmember must abstain from all participation. The 'public generally' exception typically does not apply to rate structure questions in taxing decisions.

embedding.summary: FPPC counsel confirms telephone advice to the City Attorney of Richmond regarding a councilmember's conflict of interest in a utility user tax decision affecting a corporation in which the councilmember held over $1,000 in investments. The FPPC advised mandatory abstention based on the material financial effect of the decision.
